my_hooks: Nothing better than a quiet Tuesday morning on the river, phone turned off.
Nothing better than closing your laptop on Wednesday and not opening it again until Monday.
Nothing better than watching the sun set with your family, knowing your business is running itself.

Nothing better than the smell of campfire coffee, with zero meetings on the schedule.
my_script_summary: Beat 1: Open on a beautiful, quiet, 'offline' shot (Christian by a river, packing a truck, with family).
Beat 2: The single line of hook text appears on screen.
Beat 3: The peaceful, aspirational visual continues, letting the moment breathe.

SHOW THE SYSTEM: Screen recording of the AI working in Slack or a CRM.

outliers.relevance_reason: Directly opposes "hustle" culture, advocates for systems that respect one's brain, and explicitly mentions "building lifestyle empires," perfectly aligning with FieldWork's mission.

If you’re the bottleneck in your business, you don't need more "hustle." You need a system that respects your brain.

Taki Moore is world-class at building lifestyle empires. He also has ADHD.

In this episode, we dig into why high-functioning entrepreneurs let themselves down on the "simple" stuff and how to stop trying to work like everyone else.

Real strategies for unconventional brains. No fluff.

DM "TAKI" for the link to the full conversation.

#takimoore #adhdbrain #adhdbusiness #adhdentrepreneur #neurodivergententrepreneur
outliers.why_it_worked: This copy resonates because it correctly identifies a core founder pain (being the bottleneck), dismisses the toxic 'solution' of hustle culture, and offers a smarter, more humane alternative (systems). outliers.recommended_angle: Frame the problem not just as being a bottleneck, but as building a 'golden cage' where success costs you your freedom. my_hooks: If your business runs you, you don't need another hire. You need an engine that runs itself.
If you're making more money but have less time, you don't need to work harder. You need a business that works for you.
If you're the hero in your business, you don't need a bigger cape. You need a system that makes you unnecessary.
If your 'laptop lifestyle' is just a laptop prison, you don't need more hustle. You need a system that buys back your time.
If you built a business to be free but feel trapped, the problem isn't your work ethic. It's your operating system.

Problem: The problem isn't your work ethic. It's the model.

The Shift: So I stopped trading my life for my business. I built systems instead.
The Promise: Real AI and automation that buys back your time. No hype.

outliers.relevance_reason: Discusses sales strategy, with a very subtle and indirect hint at efficiency, but no clear link to time freedom or lifestyle.
outliers.source_caption:
I locked my 9-person sales team in a cupboard for 90 days.⁠
⁠
Full pay. No calls. No DMs. No follow-up. No “just checking in…”⁠
⁠
It sounds unhinged.⁠
⁠
But it proved something important:⁠
⁠
The best prospects don’t want a sales call.⁠
⁠
They want clarity.⁠
⁠
They want confidence.⁠
⁠
They want an offer that makes sense and a simple way to say yes.⁠
⁠
First month: 12 clients.⁠
⁠
A few months later: 30 a month.⁠
⁠
Now we cap it at 45.⁠
⁠
No sales team heroics.⁠
⁠
No endless calls.⁠
⁠
No chasing people who were never gonna buy.⁠
⁠
If you want the deets on how this works, comment SALESLESS.
outliers.why_it_worked: It uses a provocative, almost unbelievable statement about a business action that seems like self sabotage. This creates an immediate open loop that the viewer needs to see closed.
outliers.recommended_angle: Christian should frame this as the time he deliberately stepped away from his business to prove that systems, not the founder's constant presence, are the key to real growth and freedom. This directly sells the FieldWork promise of 'Build Online. Live Offline.'
my_hooks: I fired myself from my own business for 90 days.
I locked myself out of my own company for an entire quarter.
I took a 60 day vacation with zero client calls. On purpose.
I told my team not to ask me a single question for 30 days.
I went completely offline for a month to see if my business would die.
my_script_summary: I fired myself from my business for 90 days.
Full pay for my team. No new projects. No client calls. No 'just checking in'.
It sounds like a great way to go broke.
But it proved something: Your business doesn't need more of you. It needs better systems.
Month one, leads went up 15%. Month two, we closed our biggest client.
No founder bottleneck. No 14 hour days. No business that dies the second you step away.
If you want the systems I used to do this, comment FIELDWORK.

outliers.relevance_reason: "363 Easy" and avoiding daily willpower/discipline directly align with escaping the grind, working smarter, and designing a sustainable work life.

Some coaches are trying to 75 Hard their content.⁠
⁠
I’m trying to 363 Easy.⁠

Because if it needs willpower and discipline every day… ⁠
⁠
I’m cooked.⁠

Better to build a system that gives you a little nudge:⁠
⁠
Notice something. Make something.⁠

Follow for more.
outliers.why_it_worked: It taps into the audience's exhaustion with 'hustle culture' by reframing a popular, intense challenge (75 Hard) into a sustainable, gentle alternative. This contrast creates immediate relatability and positions the creator as a source of relief, not another source of pressure.
outliers.recommended_angle: Frame the hustle not as a content problem, but a business model problem. The goal isn't just an easier way to post, it's an easier way to live, achieved by building an online business that funds your offline life.
my_hooks: Some founders are trying to 10x their business. I'm trying to 10x my time offline.
Most people chase another zero in their bank account. I'm chasing another day in the backcountry.
Everyone wants to build a 7-figure business. I want to build a business that runs itself for 7 days while I'm gone.
The gurus tell you to grind 16 hours a day. I'm trying to work 16 hours a week.
People are obsessed with scaling their team. I'm obsessed with scaling my freedom.

My Goal: I'm trying to 10x my time offline.

The Solution: Better to build a system that works without you.

outliers.relevance_reason: "Sales call prison" and "sad little call centre" directly address escaping the grind, and building a "Salesless system" aligns with building online income for time freedom.
outliers.source_caption:
You’ve got 3 options:⁠
⁠
Option A: stay in sales call prison forever.⁠
⁠
Option B: hire a team and accidentally become the manager of a sad little call centre.⁠
⁠
Option C: build a Salesless™ system that lets people buy without calls.⁠
⁠
A and B are available.⁠

C is better.⁠
⁠
Despite what the sales douches say, this works.⁠
⁠
Not just for me.⁠
⁠
For clients too.⁠
⁠
Hundreds of them.⁠
⁠
And yes, one of the guys publicly bagging it had privately asked me to help him fix his mess.⁠
⁠
We built the doc.⁠
⁠
He made 3 x $30k sales in 12 hours.⁠
⁠
Awkward.⁠
⁠
If you want to see how the full system works — doc, demand, deadline — comment SALESLESS.
outliers.why_it_worked: It frames the creator's solution as the only logical escape from two highly relatable, negative scenarios. The specific, dramatic anecdote about a critic provides powerful and memorable social proof.
outliers.recommended_angle: Frame the choice not just as escaping sales calls, but escaping the founder's trap entirely. The goal is to build a business that buys back your time, so you can actually live your life offline.
my_hooks: You've got 3 options as a founder.
Your business has three possible futures.
Every expert with a skill faces these three choices.
There are 3 ways this can go for you.
You can build your business in one of three ways.
my_script_summary: Option A: Stay a high paid employee in your own business. Trading hours for dollars, forever.
Option B: Hire a team and become a full time manager. More complexity, same desk.
Option C: Build automated systems that work for you. So you can get back out in the field.
Despite what the hustle gurus say, this works. Not just for me. For our clients too.
One of them just got back from 3 weeks in Costa Rica. His business grew 11% while he was gone.
If you want to see the systems he installed, comment FIELDWORK.

outliers.relevance_reason: Emphasizes reconnection with nature, friends, and a slower pace of life, aligning with the 'live offline' and 'designing life around what matters' aspects.
outliers.source_caption:
What a man really needs isn’t distraction it’s reconnection.
Reconnect with nature. Train your body hard. Spend time with good friends who bring out the best in you. Eat real food, slow down, heal properly.

Then, when your spirit feels grounded again, go find a woman you can truly build with.
outliers.why_it_worked: It addresses a deep-seated feeling of burnout and disconnection. The script offers a simple, aspirational prescription for a more grounded life, which cuts through the noise of typical hustle culture content.
outliers.recommended_angle: Position the online business not as the distraction, but as the engine that funds your reconnection. The goal isn't more work, it's building a system that buys back your time to live offline.
my_hooks: What a founder really needs isn't another hustle. It's a business that runs without them.
The goal isn't more screen time. It's more time in the field.
You don't need another productivity hack. You need a system that buys back your time.
Most founders are building a prison. We're building an escape hatch.
The problem isn't your work ethic. It's a business model that costs you your life.
my_script_summary: What a founder really needs isn't more distraction. It's a system for reconnection.
Get back in the field. Spend real time with your family. Eat dinner without your phone.
Climb a mountain on a Tuesday. Disconnect completely. Heal properly.
Then, build the online business that funds that life. Not the other way around.

outliers.relevance_reason: Defines a lifestyle focused on well-being, nature, and strong relationships, aligning with 'designing life around what matters' and 'live offline'.
outliers.source_caption:
Being “High test” to us isn’t about ego. 
It’s about how you live.

Getting sunlight. Training hard. Sauna, cold plunges, good food, good people. 
Respecting women. Calling your mum. Looking after your mates. 
Getting out in nature. Pushing yourself. Becoming harder to break and easier to love.

This isn’t about being better than anyone else. 
It’s about building a life that makes you feel alive and bringing good people along for the ride.

Thx my boy for the Inspo - @oscar14rush
outliers.why_it_worked: It reframes a potentially toxic term ('High test') into something wholesome and aspirational. It connects with a desire for a meaningful life defined by action and character, not just status.
outliers.recommended_angle: Redefine what a 'successful founder' looks like. It is not about endless hustle, but about building systems online specifically to enable a rich, present life offline.
my_hooks: Building online isn't about escaping your life.
Real wealth isn't about the number in your bank account.
Being a founder isn't about working 18 hour days.
The 'laptop lifestyle' isn't about the laptop.
An online business isn't a replacement for your life.
my_script_summary: Building online isn't about escaping your life.
It's about funding it.
Morning coffee with your kids. Weekdays on the river. Taking that trip you always talked about. Leaving your phone in the truck.
Building systems that work for you. Installing AI that handles the follow up. Selling your expertise, not your time.
This isn't about getting rich to show off.
It's about building a business that buys back your time, so you can spend it on what actually matters.

outliers.relevance_reason: Focuses on coaching business strategy and sales, not FieldWork's core message.
outliers.source_caption:
Part 3 of 5 — You do not have to force pay-in-full to build a great coaching business.⁠
⁠
A lot of people treat the sale like the finish line.⁠
⁠
It’s not.⁠
⁠
The sale is the start.⁠
⁠
If your business is built around real retention, real results, and real relationships… then the goal isn’t just to collect cash.⁠
⁠
It’s to create customers who stay.⁠
⁠
People who keep getting value.⁠
⁠
People who keep winning.⁠
⁠
People who don’t want to leave because what you do actually works.⁠
⁠
That changes how you think about pricing.⁠
⁠
That changes how you think about renewal.⁠
⁠
That changes how you think about the whole business model.⁠
⁠
There isn’t one right way to structure offers.⁠
⁠
There’s only the way that creates the kind of business you actually want to own.⁠
⁠
Follow for part 4 tomorrow — we’re talking about why gatekeeping your best stuff is ancient history.
outliers.why_it_worked: It refutes a common, stressful piece of industry advice and gives founders permission to build a business that aligns with their values, not just a popular tactic.
outliers.recommended_angle: Christian should apply the same structure to the hustle culture dogma. Argue that the goal isn't just doing the work, but building systems that do the work for you, which is the core of FieldWork.
my_hooks: You do not have to be 'always on' to build a great online business.
You do not have to trade more of your time to make more money.
You do not have to answer every DM yourself to build real relationships.
You do not have to choose between growing your business and having a life.
You do not have to sit at a desk all day to serve your people well.
my_script_summary: Most people treat their personal effort as the business's main asset.
It's not.
Your systems are.
The goal isn't just to do the work. It's to build an engine that does the work for you.
An engine that runs while you're on a flight. An engine that works while you're on a hike.
That changes how you think about your time. That changes how you think about scale.
It lets you build a business online, so you can actually live your life offline.
